questions.question: Explain the three properties that a distributed mutual exclusion algorithm must satisfy.
keyPoints: Safety: At most one process can be in critical section at any time
Liveness: Every request for CS is eventually granted (no starvation)
Ordering: Requests are honored in the order they were made (based on timestamps)

questions.question: Describe Lamport's Logical Clock with its two rules.
keyPoints: Rule 1: A process increments its logical clock before sending an event (Li = Li + 1)
Rule 2: When receiving a message with timestamp T, update: Li = max(Li, T) + 1
Purpose: Establishes partial ordering of events in distributed systems

questions.question: Compare the message complexity of Ricart-Agrawala and Maekawa algorithms.
keyPoints: Ricart-Agrawala: 2(N-1) messages per CS entry (N-1 REQUEST + N-1 REPLY)
Maekawa: O(√N) messages per CS entry (uses quorum-based approach)
Trade-off: Maekawa is more complex but more efficient in message count

questions.question: List two advantages and two disadvantages of the Token Ring algorithm.
keyPoints: Advantages: Simple to implement, guaranteed fairness (each process gets token in order)
Disadvantages: Token loss can cause starvation, high latency (may wait for token even when CS is free), token regeneration overhead

questions.question: Explain how deferred replies work in the Ricart-Agrawala algorithm.
keyPoints: When a process receives REQUEST while in CS, it defers the reply
Defers by adding the request to a queue
After exiting CS, sends REPLY to all queued requests in timestamp order
Ensures mutual exclusion is maintained

questions.question: What are the limitations of centralized mutual exclusion in distributed systems?
keyPoints: Single point of failure - coordinator crash halts entire system
Scalability issues - coordinator becomes bottleneck
Latency - every request must go through coordinator
No fault tolerance - cannot handle coordinator failure

questions.question: Explain the concept of quorum in Maekawa's mutual exclusion algorithm.
keyPoints: Quorum is a subset of processes from which permission must be obtained
Each process has a request set (quorum) of size approximately √N
Any two quorums must intersect (to ensure mutual exclusion)
Reduces messages from N-1 to approximately √N per CS entry
